Leo 磊 Su
3mo  Edited
Is the Matthew Effect in humanoid robotics getting more obvious?
Today, Apptronik  announced a $520M funding round, bringing total funding to $1B. 
I haven’t done a full research yet, but in the past few months
 • Skild AI  AI raised $1.4B  
 • Figure closed $1B  
 • In China, LimX Dynamics  raised $200M and Galaxy raised $300M 
 • In Europe, NEURA Robotics  Robotics secured €120M  
... 
Funding rounds are getting bigger and more concentrated.
When capital flows at this scale, it creates barriers to entry: 
Access to talent, training infrastructure, supply chains, and deployment partnerships.
Will smaller new entrants still have room to compete?

Alberto Muñoz
3mo
As in many technological domains, long-term value is not defined by the center of the complexity distribution but by its tails. The market is already showing signs of saturation, with many humanoid systems optimized for visibility, demonstrations, and short-lived spectacle. While these efforts have advanced actuation and balance, their commercial differentiation is rapidly diminishing, and investment concentration is outpacing the creation of durable economic ecosystems.
The true opportunity lies in the upper tail: humanoid robots capable of useful, repeatable, and economically grounded work, and of sustaining a viable chain of value creation. Systems that can operate in real workflows, manipulate tools, adapt to unstructured environments, and contribute meaningfully to productivity remain far from saturated and still offer significant room for innovation and long-horizon investment. My experience in the XPRIZE AAC underscored a key limitation: teleoperation, though tempting as a shortcut, ultimately masks rather than solves the core challenges of autonomy. The fundamental bottleneck today is dexterous manipulation. Dexterity cannot be achieved through brute force or improvisation—no one solves a Rubik’s Cube by random motion.

Val Kamenski
3mo
The $1B+ players will go after general-purpose platforms, but smaller startups that really nail one specific use case (warehouse logistics, eldercare, agriculture, etc.) can still build super defensible businesses. It’ll probably feel a lot like the early days of OpenAI: plenty of hallucinations, weird edge cases, and unexpected failures in the real world.
At the same time, open-source “general robotics” models will show up, and that’s great news for smaller teams. It means you can compete by combining those foundations with deep, domain-specific understanding of the niche you’re in.
That said, robotics, especially humanoids, is definitely not for everyone. There’s that saying: for software startup you need millions, for robotics you need billions. 😁

Robert Little
3mo
Yes, I can see how these massive funding rounds can take the air out of the room for smaller companies.
The risk with large capital raises is that speed becomes the mandate. Scale fast. Deliver fast. Pick a direction and go. But if that technology path turns out to be wrong, it’s hard to pivot. A lot of money, talent, and infrastructure are already committed to that solution.
Smaller companies have a different advantage.
Watch where the well-funded players go. Learn from it. Then build around it.
Don’t copy. Be unique.

text:
Humanoid Robotics Weekly
21 followers
6d
LAST WEEK'S THEME: (12-18 May 2026) 
The dominant pattern of the week is the rewriting of what counts as evidence. Figure's fifty-hour livestream pulled the autonomy bar from "a fluid edited clip" to "a public, continuous, observable shift," and the responses from Agility Robotics, Boston Dynamics partners and Chinese vendors are now being judged against that bar. The endurance question is no longer something a vendor can decline to engage with, and the controlled-environment caveat does not stop the competitive pressure from spreading. 
In parallel, the Humanoid (UK) / Schaeffler deal and the Persona AI / Under Armour collaboration both point at a quieter, more important shift. Industrial backers are increasingly playing both customer and supplier inside the same agreement, and the soft-goods, actuator and deployment conversations are converging into single contracts. The humanoid supply chain is no longer a discrete topic of investor decks; it is becoming the actual product. The Chinese vendors meanwhile keep widening their product surfaces ahead of their listings, with Unitree's piloted mecha and AGIBOT's Hong Kong positioning both pointing at the same calculation: the public-markets story has to be larger than the biped on stage
